transcript: No, it's not a joke. League is good. And I wouldn't be making this video if I didn't have good reasons to believe it. A little over a year ago, the game was in a pretty sore spot, with many content creators and players quitting the game, and you'd often see videos like League is dying, or quitting League of Legends, that sort of thing. I'm guilty of this as well. I even used this little fire effect for extra clickbait, but now things have changed. That sparkle of hope for the future of the game is back in full force. So, why exactly? It's a matter of direction. How the game is right now, at this second, matters less than where the game is going. And the place it's going sounds really enticing, in my opinion. In case you've missed it, this series of tweets by the current director of League, Pulyu, talks about the direction that the game will follow for the foreseeable future. To summarize, the current roadmap for 2024 is a collection of different things without a long term plan, but that do steer the game in the right direction that they want to take it for 2025. Fun over fare, change over inertia, journey before destination, his own words. The phrase makes it quite clear that this game is going into a more casual friendly direction than previous years, putting less of a focus on high elo, balancing and esports, and more on the average player, who may I remind you, is between gold and platinum elo, if they even play ranked at all. I picked Diamond 3 in Season 13, basically at the very top of low elo, so I do care about the competitive balanced aspect of the game, but I can't deny it, this new direction seems pretty exciting. The overall point, I think, is to steer the playerbase away from what's meta and more towards what's fun. I put both in quotation marks because a lot of people, me included, enjoy playing the meta. I find it fun to try hard to win, despite my questionable champion choices and macro, and fun in quotation marks because not everyone enjoys a super chaotic game where things are changing all the time. Some people do prefer a more balanced, fair game, even if it is a little bit stale at times. I'll leave the link to the thread in the description if you want to read the whole thing yourself and form your own opinion on it. It's pretty vague, especially when it comes to the League will change forever in 2025, but it does get the imagination running. If we were to compare League's new direction, I think a pair of good games to compare it to would be Overwatch and Rainbow Six Siege, because despite being first-person shooters, they have a lot of similarities with League as team-based competitive games. Overwatch launched as a 6v6 hero shooter back in 2016, which was literally 24 years ago, and when I saw the trailers, I didn't even know this was a game. I thought, wow, a new Pixar IP. That was the draw. That was the appeal. The characters, the cool cinematics, that sort of thing. Now, I don't play a lot of Overwatch, but I do have a friend that does. One thing that made the game more casual when it released was the no-role or hero limits. Meaning, you didn't need two tanks, two damage dealers and two supports, or one tank in Overwatch 2. Imposing that limit did kill some of the more wacky strategies and team compositions. Like, can you imagine a 6 D.Va comp? Overwatch 2 could never. So you could do things like playing Kiriko as a flanker, because let's be real, she can hit really hard. But nowadays if you Q as damage, you play as damage. So you can't just use a support like that. Hero kits also became a little less unique over the years. Torbjorn's turret became less of a TF2 engineer type thing, and now you just kind of throw it and forget about it until the cooldown comes back. Whereas before, you had to hit it with a wrench to upgrade it and all, you actually had to care for your turret. Symmetra was also much more unique, since she was a damage support hybrid, with a deployable shield and more powerful sentries, but less straight up damage than she does now. Doomfist is a very particular tragedy, where before he was a super unique damage dealer who relied on short range and melee abilities to deal damage, and now he's just another diver tank like Winston, mostly relying on the boring ass shotgun damage. He's also one of the few, or maybe the only hero, where they made his whole theme and visual design before the actual kit, which was made around that. It's kinda similar to Viego, where his weapon was in the game for a long time, and people were wondering, when will we get that guy as a playable character? And that was one of the aspects that made him one of the most unique heroes in the game. I said it once, and I'll say it again. Overwatch 2 could never. Neither could Rainbow Six Siege. At release, it had no intentions of being a casual game. It was a tactical shooter, which, while not exactly realistic like a proper Milsim like Arma 3 or something like Ready or Not, it was designed and marketed towards a hardcore audience from the start, so it didn't make big waves like Overwatch did, nor did it become a mainstay game like League. And at launch, it was very rough, with lots of bugs, cheaters and an overall lot of jank that almost killed the game on arrival. Now the revival that they did for the game over the first year was phenomenal to see, but it didn't change the main core reason why people burnt out on it. It's a very stressful game. Every match, whether it's ranked, quick play, casual or unranked, you're always sweating bullets because that's how the game is supposed to be played. Any single shot could vaporize you, so you could get shot through two walls, you could get shot through a tiny hole 15 meters away that you couldn't even see, you could be stalked by a drone at any given moment and you wouldn't know it, just like in real life. And so, it's a very draining game to play. Then on top of that is Ubisoft's focus on maintaining the competitive balance of the game by, well, nerfing everything with a nailed bat. I think it was after year 2 or so that the balancing started to focus a lot more on straight nerfs rather than buffs and reworks. So operators like Sledge, Mute, Jager and Melusi got some nerfs which, while somewhat justified due to their crazy pick rates, just felt really bad to play with. That's what it was, a lot of feel bad for playing these operatorfs, and people, me included, were pretty sick of it. So let's circle back to League. Those tweets from Director Pudiu make me a lot more optimistic about the future. I don't know how it is in other countries other than South Korea, but at least in Brazil, League is THE mainstay competitive team game. Most people you ask around on the street have no clue about Rainbow Six Siege or Overwatch, but at least some of them will know FIFA, Call of Duty and League of Legends. Those are the big three. And I can assure you, League is not there because the Brazilian playerbase is super good and competitive at the game. If you watch Worlds, you'd know. It's the casual appeal. Having cool looking champions with cool cinematics, having a social experience with your team of five going against another team of five in a free-to-play game that practically any old PC can run. That is what brings in new players, what brings in excitement to see what Riot has in store. And with this game direction that we have now, you can be sure that League is very far from dying or stagnating anytime soon. All it takes is a quick look at the recent PvBE changes during version 14.4. Runan's Hurricane being usable on melee champions brings out a lot of completely busted out of this world interactions, like Master Yi using Q every nanosecond, Xingjiao's healing putting Briar to shame, that kind of thing. But isn't that what the PBE is for? In my opinion we should have more unhinged things in the PBE, we should be testing these schizo-experiments like that more often, just to see what happens. And if it's too busted, just don't bring it to live servers. Runan's changes got all reverted, and they aren't gonna hit live servers. But seeing all of these stupid interactions with PBE changes makes me more excited to see more of this stuff in the future, not less. Yeah, sure, there are some anxieties, like Vanguard anti-cheat making the game unplayable on certain systems, or privacy concerns that naturally rise with a kernel-level anti-cheat. But I will not pretend like I even know what a kernel is, so I'll refrain from commenting. And I play Genshin, so China already has my data anyway. But it would be dishonest to make this video and not talk about some things tangential to League. Mainly the layoffs that happened earlier this year, which had Riot let go of around 11% of their total workforce, Riot Forge and surprisingly Legends of Runeterra were announced to be discontinued, and the designer of Smolder, also apparently being let go, makes this secret voice line a lot harsher in hindsight. So even if this all doesn't directly affect League of Legends' quality, it does plant that seed in your head. Is everything okay over there? Should I be worried? And hopefully those questions can be answered in the upcoming devlogs and streams. I also want to give a shoutout to my boy Wilson, because if I hadn't seen his comment on a late Monday night, this video would not have been made. That's it, I made a positive video about League, that is one hell of a thing to take off the bucket list.
